html:
Bike24 Holding AG: The Bike Bust Survivor Turning Into a Cash Generator

Bike24 is still labeled as a broken post-COVID bicycle retailer. That label is stale. The company just reported 2025 revenue of €289.1 million, up 27.7%, adjusted EBITDA of €14.5 million, up 173%, and free cash flow of €16.0 million. Q1 2026 then showed the recovery is not fading: revenue rose 21.9% to €70.7 million, adjusted EBITDA tripled to €1.8 million, active customers rose 24.5%, and returning-customer orders reached about 71%. Management kept 2026 guidance at €318-332 million of revenue and €16-20 million of adjusted EBITDA.

The market is missing the operating leverage because GAAP earnings still look ugly. Bike24 has heavy non-cash amortization from legacy purchase-accounting assets: 2025 D&A was €16.8 million versus only €1.9 million of capex. That makes the P/E screen useless. The better lens is cash earnings after the inventory crisis. On the latest €2.80 share price, Bike24’s market cap is about €124 million, with 44.17 million shares outstanding. Including bank debt, cash, and lease liabilities, EV is roughly €145 million, or about 8x 2026 guided EBITDA. Excluding leases, EV is closer to €130 million, about 7x midpoint EBITDA. More importantly, if 2026-27 revenue keeps compounding while fixed costs grow slower than sales, market cap to forward owner FCF can compress toward 5-7x.

The variant perception is not “cheap retailer at 5x stale EBITDA.” It is that Bike24 is a structurally better survivor in a cycling market that has already washed out excess inventory, weak balance sheets, and irrational competitors. Wiggle/Chain Reaction entered administration in 2023 and was bought by Frasers; Internetstores/fahrrad.de/Bikester also went through insolvency and asset sales. The brands did not vanish entirely, but the old aggressive, venture-funded, discount-heavy competitive set is gone. Customer chatter after CRC/Wiggle’s collapse still points riders toward continental alternatives like Bike24, Bike-Discount, and Bike-Components. That is not a stock catalyst by itself, but it supports the idea that Bike24 retained real mindshare while competitors lost trust.

The German bicycle industry is still not healthy. ZIV’s 2025 market data showed German bicycle/e-bike retail turnover down 7.7% to €5.85 billion, with discounting still pressuring average prices. That makes Bike24’s 2025 and Q1 2026 growth more impressive. It is taking share in a soft market, not merely riding a broad industry rebound. The key proof point is breadth: Q1 DACH revenue rose 20.7%, localized markets rose 30.0%, full bikes rose 27.2%, and parts/accessories/clothing rose 20.8%. Poland and Finland, launched in February 2025, grew 109% and 59%, respectively. This is not one lucky category.

The balance sheet is no longer distressed. Cash was €18.2 million at March 31, 2026, bank utilization was €24.0 million, the facility was extended to April 2028, and the company is scheduled to repay €4 million per year. The risk is not solvency. The risk is whether management converts cash flow into per-share value instead of simply carrying liquidity. The AGM buyback authorization exists, and a buyback at 1x book and a single-digit FCF multiple would be highly accretive, but there is not yet a clear capital-return habit.

The upside case depends on Bike24 proving that 5% EBITDA margins are not the ceiling. At €325 million revenue, management’s €18 million midpoint EBITDA guide is only a 5.5% margin. If revenue reaches €350-370 million in 2027 and the business earns 7-8% EBITDA margins, adjusted EBITDA becomes €25-30 million. Given low capex and declining interest burden, that can translate into high-teens to low-20s millions of equity FCF. A niche European e-commerce leader with recurring enthusiast demand, high returning-customer mix, improving inventory turns, and positive FCF should not trade at 5-7x that cash flow.

The main bear case is that 2025-26 growth is a temporary restocking and weather benefit, not durable share gain. Q1 had an early season and favorable demand conditions, while the industry still has price pressure. Inventory rose to €80.8 million in Q1 for peak-season availability, so a poor Q2/Q3 could leave Bike24 with too much stock again. Liquidity is also a real risk: this is a microcap, the shareholder base includes Riverside at 31.3% and Rocket Internet at 10.1%, and daily trading volume is thin. A holder exit can move the stock more than fundamentals.

But the risk/reward is still attractive because the business no longer needs heroic assumptions. It only needs Q2/H1 2026 to confirm that Q1 was broad share gain rather than a one-off. The next earnings date shown by Investing.com is August 12, 2026. A maintained or raised 2026 guide, positive peak-season cash flow, or any buyback signal can force investors to value Bike24 on current-cycle cash earnings instead of old bicycle-bust losses.

Current price: €2.80.

Bear target: €1.90, down 32%. Revenue growth slows sharply, 2026 EBITDA lands near €14-15 million, inventory absorbs cash, and the market keeps applying a deep liquidity discount.

Base target: €4.75, up 70%. Bike24 delivers 2026 guidance, exits the year with lower net bank debt, and investors value 2027 FCF at roughly 10-11x.

Bull target: €7.00, up 150%. Revenue compounds into the mid-€300 millions, EBITDA reaches €28-32 million, free cash flow exceeds €22 million, and a buyback or dividend proves minority shareholders will participate in the recovery.
